---
title: "Rung 5 · Thoughtful iteration"
---

[Setup.]{.lead-in} Everything from rungs 1–4: a graph, a complexity read, a
structural attribution, and validation evidence.

[Move.]{.lead-in} Decide whether to trust the answer or climb back down. A high
complexity score or wide discovery disagreement is a signal to refine the DAG,
add constraints, and re-validate.

## The ladder is a loop

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Discover structure] --> B[Score complexity]
  B --> C[Structural Causal SHAP]
  C --> D[Validate vs known truth]
  D -->|drift or disagreement| A
  D -->|stable| E[Report with uncertainty]
```

## The nine-step Causal Roadmap

Iteration is disciplined by making assumptions explicit, in order: (1) the causal
question and target population; (2) the causal model (the DAG); (3) the causal
parameter (estimand); (4–6) data, identifiability, and the statistical estimand;
(7–9) estimator, sensitivity, and interpretation. **No DAG ⇒ no clear
identification strategy.**

::: {.pullquote}
"A wrong DAG is better than no DAG, because at least we can critique and refine
it." The point of the ladder is not a single climb — it is a habit of revision.
:::

## Where to go next

- Try it: the [interactive app](../app.qmd).
- Keep the references handy: the [cheatsheets](../cheatsheets/dag-vocabulary.qmd).
- Rebuild it: the [reproducibility guide](../reproducibility.qmd).
